summary: The Gemara concludes the analysis of which prohibitions override Temple service, distinguishing between tumah hutrah b'tzibur (permitted for communal service) and the perpetual fire law ('lo tichbeh') - whose prohibition against extinguishing applies even on Shabbat.

display.whats: Is ritual impurity hutrah (permitted) or dechuyah (suspended) for communal service, and what is the scope of the perpetual altar fire prohibition?
display.hint: Two key principles: (1) Communal tumah is hutrah - truly permitted, not merely suspended. This affects edge cases like a priest who becomes impure mid-service. (2) The altar fire prohibition applies continuously and is not suspended even on Shabbat. The Gemara works through both.
display.title: Truly Permitted, Not Just Tolerated: Hutrah vs. Dechuyah

learning.ahaMoment: Tumah hutrah b'tzibur: communal impurity is truly permitted, not just tolerated. The altar fire never goes out - even on Shabbat the fire is not among the 39 prohibited labors because it was never included.
learning.memoryAnchor: Hutrah = truly permitted. Dechuyah = prohibited but overridden. The practical difference shows when the impurity arose mid-service.
learning.learnerQuestion: Is communal ritual impurity truly permitted (hutrah) for Temple service, or merely tolerated and pushed aside (dechuyah) - and does the altar fire's perpetual burning ever count as violating Shabbat?
learning.coreTension: Whether communal tumah is hutrah or dechuyah has real practical stakes, such as whether a priest who becomes impure mid-service may continue, and the perpetual altar fire raises a parallel question of whether maintaining it on Shabbat is an override or was never included in the Shabbat prohibition at all.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara proves from Rav Chisda that communal tumah must be hutrah, since two would-be dechuyot would otherwise cancel each other out, and rules that the altar fire was never included among the Shabbat-prohibited labors, so tending it is not an override at all.

takeaway.text: Communal ritual impurity is hutrah (truly permitted), not merely dechuyah (suspended) - proven from the fact that competing dechuyot would otherwise cancel each other out; the altar fire's continuous burning on Shabbat is not an override, since the fire was never included in the Shabbat prohibitions to begin with.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume 'hutrah' means the prohibition was technically violated but excused, missing that it means the case was never included in the prohibition in the first place.

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Communal tumah is hutrah (permitted), not merely dechuyah (suspended) - it was never prohibited in this context.

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Proof from Rav Chisda: if dechuyah, two competing dechuyot should cancel; since they don't, it must be hutrah.

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The perpetual altar fire ('a fire shall always be burning on the altar, it shall not be extinguished') applies continuously.

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The prohibition against extinguishing the altar fire applies on Shabbat - the fire was never included in the Shabbat labor prohibitions.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: The prohibition against extinguishing the altar fire

halachic: hutrah vs dechuyah for communal tumah

misconceptions.misconception: 'Hutrah' means the prohibition technically applies but is excused or overlooked for the sake of Temple service.
misconceptions.correction: 'Hutrah' means the case was never included in the prohibition at all - it is genuinely permitted, not merely excused, which is why competing dechuyot would otherwise cancel each other out but hutrah does not.

visualizableElements.label: tumah hutrah vs tumah dechuyah - practical difference in edge cases

quizSeeds.answer: Hutrah means tumah was never prohibited in communal Temple service (truly permitted); dechuyah means it is prohibited but overridden by Temple service need. The difference appears when impurity arises mid-service.
quizSeeds.question: What is the difference between tumah hutrah and tumah dechuyah?

sugyot.finalRuling: Communal tumah is hutrah (truly permitted) for Temple service; the altar fire prohibition applies on Shabbat since the fire is not among the 39 prohibited labors.

glossary.en: Ritual impurity is permitted for communal Temple service - it is not merely tolerated but genuinely allowed.
Ritual impurity is pushed aside (overridden) for Temple service - the prohibition technically exists but is suspended.

rashiTranslations.en: The dispute whether tumah is hutrah or dechuyah in communal service has major practical implications.
If hutrah: a kohen who became impure mid-service on a communal-tumah day may finish.
If dechuyah: he must stop, since the override applies only to the initial state, not to new impurity.
Rav Chisda's proof: two dechuyot do not combine into a permission; if it were dechuyah, competing rules would block each other.
Since the communal tumah ruling holds even in complex cases, it must be hutrah.
The Gemara brings another proof from R. Yochanan about the nature of the override.
The perpetual fire: 'a fire shall always be burning on the altar' - always, including Shabbat.
'It shall not be extinguished' - even on Shabbat, the fire must not go out.
But maintaining the fire requires adding wood on Shabbat - how is this permitted?
Answer: the fire was never included in the Shabbat prohibition. 'In your settlements' excludes Temple.
This is not an override of Shabbat - it is a carve-out that was never part of Shabbat law.
Practical consequence: a priest who adds wood to the altar fire on Shabbat has not violated Shabbat.
The violation he might commit is the prohibition of extinguishing if he handled the fire improperly.
Adding wood is a mitzvah; extinguishing even one coal is a prohibition.
The Gemara concludes 46b with the perpetual fire laws fully analyzed.
Transition: 47a will begin a new Mishna about the spoon and coal pan brought to the High Priest.
